medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia for the lid-margin pain, as advised in the source note. Well tolerated and appropriate for a benign self-limiting condition.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally up to four times daily as needed (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)
medications.duration: as needed for a few days until pain settles

medications.indication_rationale: Topical chloramphenicol 1% eye ointment to the lid margin, as prescribed in the source note, to limit secondary conjunctival infection if the pustule ruptures. Evidence for topical antibiotics in an uncomplicated hordeolum is modest — warm compresses are the mainstay — so it is an acceptable adjunct rather than mandatory, and in Australia it is a pharmacist-available (OTC) preparation the patient can obtain without a prescription.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to the affected lid margin twice daily for 5 days

medications.indication_rationale: Topical ocular corticosteroids are not indicated for an acute external hordeolum and, being prescription-only agents that can worsen infection and raise intraocular pressure, should not be recommended in a telehealth self-care context.

Systemic antibiotics are not indicated for a simple, localised external hordeolum. They are reserved for spreading preseptal/orbital cellulitis — none of whose features are present — so recommending them here would be unnecessary and is an antibiotic-stewardship error.
medications.deprescribing_note: Would only become indicated if the safety-net red flags for cellulitis appeared, at which point in-person review governs prescribing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a clean, comfortably warm (not scalding) moist compress to the closed eyelid for about 10 minutes, four times a day, until the lump points and drains; gently clean the lid margin afterwards.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Sustained warmth softens the thickened, infected gland secretions and encourages the pustule to point and drain naturally — the evidence-based mainstay of hordeolum management that usually brings relief within 48-72 hours.
behavioural_change_actions.feasibility_note: The patient has ready access to warm water at home and can do this independently.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wash hands thoroughly before and after touching the eye or applying the compress, and avoid eye make-up and contact lenses until it settles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Hand and lid hygiene reduces re-inoculation and spread; the source note emphasises rigorous hand-washing.

patient_education_points.point: This is a small blocked, infected oil gland at the base of an eyelash. The main treatment is heat: warm compresses melt the blockage so it can drain naturally, and it usually starts to settle within 2-3 days.
Do NOT squeeze, pop or try to lance the lump. Squeezing can push the infection deeper — the veins around the face drain toward the brain — so let it drain on its own with warmth.
Wash your hands before and after touching your eye, and avoid rubbing it, eye make-up and contact lenses until it clears.
The whitehead often bursts and drains by itself over 2-3 days, giving quick pain relief; the leftover swelling can take 1-2 weeks to fully go, and a small painless lump may remain for a while.

Seek help urgently if the redness spreads across the whole eyelid or onto your cheek, your vision changes, you get double vision or pain moving the eye, or you develop a fever — these would need to be checked in person.

follow_up_plan.interval: Expected to settle over 1-2 weeks with warm compresses; review with your regular GP in about 3 days if it is not improving, the pain is worsening, or the swelling is spreading.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Extent and spread of lid redness/swelling, vision, presence of any painful eye movements or diplopia, whether the pustule has drained, and response to warm compresses.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If the redness spreads beyond the lid margin to the whole eyelid or cheek, vision changes, double vision or pain on eye movement develops, or a fever appears — seek urgent in-person/emergency review the same day rather than waiting for the routine review.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, self-limiting eyelid gland infection (external hordeolum) manageable via telehealth self-care — do not over-escalate a classic stye to ED/000
Advise warm compresses to the closed eyelid ~4 times daily for about 10 minutes as the mainstay of treatment
Explicitly advise NOT to squeeze, pop or lance the lump
Advise hand hygiene and avoiding rubbing the eye, make-up and contact lenses
Provide simple analgesia (e.g. paracetamol) for pain
Safety-net the cellulitis red flags: spreading redness beyond the lid margin, vision change, double vision or pain on eye movement, or fever — seek urgent in-person/ED review
Advise routine GP review in ~3 days if not improving or worsening
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending OTC/pharmacist topical chloramphenicol eye ointment as an adjunct if there is discharge — acceptable alternative, not mandatory
Directing the patient to a pharmacist for OTC treatment and self-care advice — acceptable equivalent to GP review for the routine pathway
Advising lid hygiene / warm lid massage for the underlying recurrent gland dysfunction as longer-term prevention
errors_of_omission: Not warning against squeezing or popping the lump
Not providing safety-netting for preseptal/orbital cellulitis red flags
Not advising warm compresses as first-line treatment
errors_of_commission: Escalating a classic uncomplicated stye to the emergency department or calling an ambulance (over-triage)
Advising the patient to squeeze, pop or lance the lump
Prescribing or recommending systemic antibiotics or topical corticosteroids without any cellulitis features
Falsely reassuring the patient it is harmless while giving no cellulitis safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a minor, self-limiting condition: a PASS requires self-care advice (warm compresses AND explicit do-not-squeeze) PLUS safety-netting for the preseptal/orbital cellulitis red flags. Correctly managing this at home is the right answer — over-triaging a classic stye to urgent in-person/ED is a calibration error, not a pass condition. Giving self-care with no cellulitis safety-net is a fail.
